title: AP World History: Modern DBQ Practice Exam 03

questions.prompt:
Evaluate the extent to which environmental problems encouraged new forms of global cooperation and conflict from c. 1970 to 2000.
Document 1
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Statement by a delegate from Sweden at an international environmental conference, 1972, Stockholm.
Source Type: Conference statement.
“Smoke, poisoned rivers, and chemical waste do not stop at national borders. Industrial progress has improved human life, but it has also created dangers that no country can manage alone. We must develop shared standards, scientific monitoring, and international agreements so that economic growth does not destroy the conditions of life itself.”
Sourcing cues: Delegate from an industrialized country hosting an environmental conference; emphasizes transnational regulation and scientific cooperation.
Document 2
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Speech by a delegate from India at the same international conference, 1972, Stockholm.
Source Type: Conference speech.
“We are asked to limit pollution, but many nations making these requests became wealthy through centuries of industrial use of coal, forests, minerals, and colonial resources. Poverty is also an environmental danger. A village without clean water, fuel, or land suffers as surely as a city with polluted air. Environmental policy must not become a tool by which the wealthy deny development to the poor.”
Sourcing cues: Representative of a developing country; links environmental policy to inequality, development, and postcolonial concerns.
Document 3
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Environmental monitoring summary, international scientific panel, 1985.
Source Type: Plain-text data table.
Selected Atmospheric Measurements, 1970–1985
Year Average Measured CFC Concentration Reported Ozone Layer Concern
1970 Low but rising Limited public concern
1975 Higher Scientific debate increasing
1980 Much higher Growing concern over ozone depletion
1985 Sharp increase documented Major concern after Antarctic ozone data
Note on the table: CFCs were widely used in refrigeration, aerosol sprays, and industrial processes. Scientists warned that ozone depletion could increase harmful ultraviolet radiation.
Sourcing cues: Scientific panel data; useful for showing environmental problems identified through international research.
Document 4
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Statement by executives of a chemical manufacturing association, 1987, London.
Source Type: Industry statement.
“Industry recognizes public concern about the ozone layer, but sudden bans on widely used chemicals would harm refrigeration, medicine storage, food transport, and employment. Any transition must be gradual, scientifically verified, and economically realistic. Governments should avoid measures that punish responsible producers while rewarding competitors who ignore regulations.”
Sourcing cues: Industry perspective; acknowledges environmental concerns but prioritizes economic costs, jobs, and regulation timing.
Document 5
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Description of an environmental activist poster, 1988, Toronto.
Source Type: Described poster.
The poster shows Earth as a blue sphere under a cracked transparent shield. Around the planet are drawings of aerosol cans, refrigerators, factories, and children wearing wide-brimmed hats under a bright sun. At the bottom, a slogan reads: “One Atmosphere, One Future: Ban Ozone-Destroying Chemicals.”
Sourcing cues: Activist visual source; uses emotional imagery and global symbolism to encourage public support for regulation.
Document 6
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Excerpt from a treaty summary distributed by an international organization, 1990.
Source Type: Treaty summary.
“Member states agree to phase down the production and consumption of substances identified as damaging to the ozone layer. Industrialized countries shall begin reductions earlier. A financial mechanism will assist developing countries in obtaining substitute technologies. Scientific review panels will recommend future adjustments as evidence changes.”
Sourcing cues: International organization summary; reflects negotiated compromise between environmental goals, scientific expertise, and development concerns.
Document 7
Author/Origin/Year/Place: Interview with a Brazilian environmental official after debates over Amazon deforestation, 1992, Rio de Janeiro.
Source Type: Interview excerpt.
“Foreign governments speak of the forest as if it belongs to humanity in general, but it lies within our national territory. We accept the need to protect biodiversity and indigenous communities, yet we cannot ignore roads, farms, minerals, and jobs. Cooperation is welcome when it respects sovereignty. Pressure without resources will be seen as interference.”
Sourcing cues: Government official from a country with major rainforest resources; emphasizes sovereignty, development, conservation, and international pressure.
questions.scoringGuide:
Total Points: 7
Thesis / Claim — 1 point: Earns the point by making a historically defensible claim that evaluates the extent to which environmental problems encouraged global cooperation and conflict from c. 1970 to 2000. A strong thesis should establish a line of reasoning, such as arguing that environmental problems created new institutions and treaties but also intensified conflicts over development, sovereignty, and economic responsibility.
Contextualization — 1 point: Earns the point by describing a broader historical context relevant to global environmental politics, such as postwar industrial growth, decolonization, the rise of international organizations, scientific monitoring, consumer society, Cold War-era global conferences, globalization of production, or growing public activism.
Evidence from the Documents — 2 points: Earns 1 point by accurately using the content of at least three documents to address the prompt. Earns 2 points by using at least six documents to support an argument about environmental cooperation, scientific research, treaty-making, activism, industry resistance, development concerns, or sovereignty conflicts.
Evidence Beyond the Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by using at least one specific piece of relevant outside evidence not found in the documents, such as the creation of the United Nations Environment Programme, the Montreal Protocol, Earth Day, the Chernobyl disaster, Bhopal disaster, Green parties, climate change negotiations, the Kyoto Protocol, biodiversity agreements, or debates over sustainable development.
Sourcing / Analysis of Documents — 1 point: Earns the point by explaining how or why the point of view, purpose, audience, or historical situation of at least three documents is relevant to the argument. Effective examples include explaining why developing-country delegates emphasized poverty and inequality, why industry groups warned against sudden bans, why activists used emotional global imagery, or why governments with rainforests emphasized sovereignty.
Complexity — 1 point: Earns the point by demonstrating a more complex understanding of environmental politics. This may include explaining that environmental problems were global in physical effect but unequal in responsibility and capacity, that science encouraged cooperation while economic interests produced conflict, or that environmental agreements required compromises between industrialized and developing states.
questions.modelGuidance: A strong DBQ could argue that environmental problems encouraged new forms of global cooperation through scientific monitoring, conferences, treaties, and financial mechanisms, but also produced conflict over development, industrial costs, sovereignty, and historical responsibility. Documents 1, 3, 5, and 6 can support arguments about scientific and institutional cooperation. Documents 2, 4, and 7 can support arguments about conflict over economic inequality, regulation, and national sovereignty. Outside evidence might include the Montreal Protocol, UNEP, Earth Day, Bhopal, Chernobyl, the Kyoto Protocol, or sustainable development debates. Strong sourcing could analyze the contrasting perspectives of industrialized states, developing countries, industry groups, activists, and resource-rich governments.
